sections.subject: Citizenship

entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): Plutarch: Grades 6-8, Canadian Government: Grade 7, Ethics: Part 1, Current Events: Grades 5-8, Community Read Alouds: Grades 1-12

About Plutarch: Grades 6-8
Plutarch’s Lives is a collection of biographies written 2,000 years ago, which has influenced prominent thinkers from Montaigne to Bacon to Shakespeare. Plutarch’s stories are often difficult to read (and are therefore often dropped by teachers), but their value to help students evaluate the characteristics that define a good citizen cannot be overstated. The lesson plans will contain additional support to what is already included in the Anne White guides.

About Current Events: Grades 5-8
Students are exposed to current events by reading from a variety of sources and having age-appropriate conversations with teachers. Topics such as scientific discoveries, the arts, and human interest stories are generally suitable.

About Canadian Government: Grade 7
Students learn about the electoral process, the different aspects of parliamentary government, the Canadian legal system, and the concept of citizenship. The will spend the final term learning about the structure of our local and provincial authorities.

About Community Read Alouds: Grades 1-12
This is meant to be a read-aloud for all ages to enjoy together in the afternoon at least 1 time a week. We encourage you to choose a book that is specific to your region. However, we will offer some suggestions in the Grades 1-3 Literature lessons and Grades 4+ Citizenship lessons. The suggested book titles will be available June 1st in the lesson plans.

About Ethics: Part 1
One of Mason’s main pillars is that the student is born a “person.” Ourselves is the book in which she defines that term, and it is the only volume she wrote directly to students. The book is divided into two parts. We use Ourselves Part 1 in Grades 7-9 and Part 2 in Grades 10-11, as Mason did in her own schools. In these books, students will explore characteristics shared by all Image-Bearers and learn how to guard against good servants becoming bad masters with Charlotte Mason's text written directly to students.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
Plutarch: Grades 6-8
Students in 5th grade or new to Plutarch may want to take the Beginner's Track, which covers the life of Publicola over two terms. It is advisable to have students read Beesly’s Stories From the History of Rome and complete one year of Shakespeare before beginning Plutarch.

Ethics: Part 1
Ourselves by Charlotte Mason is scheduled over five years. In a multi-age context, students can jump into the book wherever the class is reading. Otherwise, students should begin the book in Part 1.

entries.content:
Permission to print for non-commercial use. See Alveary group use policy to use lessons
in a group context.

LINKS: Click text or scan the QR code in the top corner of the lesson plan pages to view
online resources associated with the lessons.

Responsibility for previewing all links rests with the teacher. All links were checked at the
time of publication; however, websites change frequently and may contain objectionable
content. Please report broken links by contacting us through our website.

Citizenship: Grade 7 (Canada)
☐ Make sure your student sets up a Citizenship Notebook. They will need a 3-ring binder, lined notebook paper, and 5 Tabbed Dividers. You can read more about how to set one up and suggestions for various sections in the document in Quick Links. 

☐ As desired, plan for a read aloud time. Suggestions are provided in the Community Read Aloud Quick Link, but local favorites are highly recommended whenever possible.

☐ Make a Calendar of Events for Current Events lessons by drawing a calendar grid in a notebook (or print a blank calendar from the internet).

Plutarch: Grades 6-8
☐ Watch the video in Quick Links about teaching Plutarch.

The World and Everything In It

blocks.text:
Plutarch: Teachers will find it helpful to preread the entire Life first, and then preread the day’s passage before each lesson. Mark any passages that should be omitted due to content. (In Anne White’s Plutarch Project books, the recommended source for this course, objectionable content has already been omitted.)

Current Events: Preview the news source for suitability.

Most other citizenship topics don't require additional prep.
Connect back to the previous lesson. (Optional for Current Events).

For news sources, show students how to scan the headlines to get an overview first and then choose a story to read. 

For Plutarch or other books: Use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to prepare students to receive the story as relevant. You may talk about one or two unfamiliar words that will be crucial to understanding. It is not necessary to cover all unfamiliar words.

Read the selection assigned or the news story chosen. For rich, dense, or more challenging books such as Ourselves and Plutarch, it is a good idea to read them aloud. Have students keep an eye out for things to add to their Citizenship Notebook, Book of Centuries, and/or Commonplace book.
Have the students narrate orally or in writing.

Give students an opportunity to share their thoughts on the story and any connections made. For current events, look up where events occurred on a map and ask questions to help students discern the quality of the piece. (e.g., Are there sources cited? Is there an obvious bias? If so, what do other authors have to say on this subject? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?) Consider reading an alternative perspective.
Have students record important ideas in their Citizenship Notebooks.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Country of Mansoul
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Charlotte Mason, the author of this text, felt that the available material on formal formation during her time was severely lacking; what was available she considered unsuitably preachy and uninteresting. She felt compelled to write an approachable but challenging alternative, which is the text for this course: Ourselves. 

Mason believed that before we could fully consider our duties to God, country, and others, we must first know and begin to understand ourselves, including the possibilities and risks inherent in all of us. Self-knowledge, in her view, was the inevitable prerequisite to self-direction, which is why she aptly named the first and second "Books" of Ourselves after each. 

In order to present these ideas in an interesting way, she used the allegory of a country and its government. In her own words: "The realised self of each of us is a distressfully poor thing, and yet upon its insight and its action depends the redemption of that greater self, whose limitations no man has discovered. It is, to use a figure, as the relation between a country and its government. The country is ever greater than the governing body; and yet, for its development, the former must depend upon the latter." 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Ch.I p.1-2 "Do ye not" - "be a hero."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Begin drawing a map of Mansoul using information from today’s reading.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
londes: an old English word for lands

lessons.title: 15m Current Events: Grades 5-8 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Tracker
⬚  ⬚  ⬚  ⬚  ⬚  

News source of your choice
PREP: Preview news source

➜ INTRO
Let's scan the headlines and see what is in the news. What sounds interesting to you? Let's make sure we read a variety of stories. (sports, arts and leisure, politics, op-eds, world news, local news).

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
News story

• What are your thoughts? What sources do they cite? Is there an obvious bias? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?

➜ LOCATE
Look at a map and find where the events occurred

➜ NOTEBOOK
Write the date and summary of the event in the Current Events section of your Citizenship Notebook.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 2
lessons.body:
Country of Mansoul
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ RECAP
In the last lesson, we were introduced to the country of Mansoul. How would you describe it in your own words? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Ch.I p.3-4 "There are libraries" - "rich and beautiful."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Continue work on map of Mansoul.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
cricket: a bat and ball game that originated and is popular in England

• COMPOSITION
How is Mansoul like a rich and beautiful land?

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 3
lessons.body:
Perils of Mansoul
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
What do you remember about the culture of Mansoul from our last reading? How is it similar to where you live? How is it different? 

Today, we will begin a new chapter called The Perils of Mansoul. A peril is a serious or immediate danger. Let’s see what dangers are lurking for Mansoul.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Ch.II p.5-6 "You are thinking" - "fires and lights."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
incendiary: a device or strategy intended to start a fire

Föhn: a strong, warm air that comes down off the Alps and is known to both melt snow and spread wildfires

• COMPOSITION
What are ways a flame could come from a foreign land?

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 4
lessons.body:
Perils of Mansoul
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
What perils put the country of Mansoul at risk? How? What, if anything, could be done to prevent them? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Ch.II p.6-8 "Sometimes there is" - "to its duties."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
ministry: a specific department within the government
 
Prime Minister: the head of the British government 

• COMPOSITION
Tell about some of the Perils of Mansoul and tell which are the most perilous for you.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 5
lessons.body:
Government of Mansoul
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
In the last lesson, we read about several more perils to Mansoul: Plague, Flood, Famine, Discord, and Darkness. What causes these perils, and what are some of the consequences? 

Today, we will be introduced to the government of Mansoul. In the same way that every country must have a form of government, so, too, must a person have an internal "government" that rules them.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Ch.III p.9-10 "I must give" - "a human being."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
esquire: similar to a page, a lower servant or assistant to a higher government office
 
lord: the formal title for a ruler over a particular land or territory

exchequer /eks CHEK er/: the name of the British government department responsible for the collection and management of taxes and revenue; similar to a Treasury 

minister: the head of a specific department (ministry) within the government

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 6
lessons.body:
Esquires of the Body: Hunger
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
What do you remember about the government of Mansoul? Why is it important to understand these powers within a person?

In today's reading, you'll hear the word esquire. In Mason’s time, an esquire was often one who received a title (such as “lord”) for service to the king. Sometimes they worked for the good of the kingdom, but other times to gain an advantage over others. How would that affect the kingdom? In this book, Mason gives the name “esquire” to the appetites of the body. As we read, keep this in mind.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.I Ch.I p.11-13 "We will first" - "two hours long."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Start a chart of the government of Mansoul that includes the King, the Prime Minister, and the Esquires. As you read, tell what their duties are and how each has the potential to damage the kingdom of Mansoul when they try to become too powerful.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS

pudding: dessert

hampers: packages of food

• COMPOSITION

lessons.body:
Esquires: Restlessness and Rest
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Describe what you remember about Thirst. How can Thirst be a source of danger for Mansoul? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.I Ch.III p.18-20 "I hardly know" - "work or play."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Continue adding to government chart.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
daemon: As Mason uses it here, it refers to the cruel, evil, and/or destructive manifestation of a quality or trait.

• COMPOSITION
Describe sloth and what its outcome can be in the body.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
Pages of the Body: Five Senses
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Last term, we read about the government of Mansoul. Describe what you remember about its King, its Prime Minister, and its Esquires. 

Today, we will read about “Pages.” These are the assistants, or servants, to the Esquires.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.I Ch.V p.24-26 "The Esquires" - "fresh or fusty"

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Start a list for each Page and their duties.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION
Tell how to keep Smell "in good working order."

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
Pages of the Body: Five Senses
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Describe how Taste is a useful Page to the body. What happens when we pamper it? 
Why is Smell described as lazy? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.I Ch.V p.26-28 "There are five" - "cause of the pain."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Continue adding to lists on Pages.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
Spartan boy: This refers to a story from Sparta where a young boy caught a live fox and hid it in his robes. The fox began to bite and claw him, but the boy was determined to show bravery and refused to move or ask for help until he died from his wounds.

⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Describe how Touch is a good servant to the body, but a bad master. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.I Ch.V p.28-30 "But, on the other" - "can distinguish."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Continue adding to lists on Pages.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
mustard poultice: a paste often used to treat chest congestion in Mason's time. If left too long, it can irritate the skin.

drawing room: similar to a living room

James Simpson: a Scottish doctor who first used chloroform as an anesthetic

• COMPOSITION
"Do you know how Eyes and No-Eyes went out for a walk?" Tell what followed in the reading.

➜ INTRO
Let's scan the headlines and see what is in the news. What sounds interesting to you? Let's make sure we read a variety of stories. (sports, arts and leisure, politics, op-eds, world news, local news).

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS
News story

• What are your thoughts? What sources do they cite? Is there an obvious bias? Are arguments supported by evidence? Is that evidence credible?

➜ LOCATE
Look at a map and find where the events occurred

➜ NOTEBOOK
Write the date and summary of the event in the Current Events section of your Citizenship Notebook.

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 17
lessons.body:
Ourselves
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
How are Sight and Hearing sources of joy? Explain the statement, "The more we listen, the more we hear." 

Today, we will begin a new part of our book. We will be reading about all of the parts that comprise our whole selves: our bodies, minds, hearts, and souls.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.I p.33-34 "When we think" - "the Government."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Create a chart with four columns labeled Body, Mind, Heart, and Soul. Record important ideas in the appropriate column after each reading.

lessons.body:

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 18
lessons.body:
My Lord Intellect
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Why is it important to understand our "inner" selves? Explain the differences between Self-Control, Self-Knowledge, and Self-Reverence.

Today, we will read about a new member of the government, the Lord Intellect (equivalent to the Foreign Secretary in the U.K., the Secretary of State in the U.S., or the Minister of Foreign Affairs in Canada). This official's role is to talk with representatives from other countries and work out problems peacefully.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.II p.35-37 "To begin with" - "King had gone."

• CITIZENSHIP NOTEBOOK
Add the Foreign Secretary to chart of government officials started in Lesson 6.
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
kinetoscope: a type of early motion picture device designed for one person

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 19
lessons.body:
My Lord Intellect
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Why is Intellect given the title the "Foreign Secretary" of Mansoul? Describe his job duties.   

Today, we will read about Philosophy, which is the study of knowledge and reality. How do you know something is true?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.II p.37-39 "We see, too" - "ease for Mansoul."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION
Explain how Imagination can be a good companion in travels OR Tell about the land of Mathematics.

➜ INTRO

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.II p.39-41 "Perhaps the least" - "by this test."

lessons.body:
My Lord Intellect
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Describe the Kingdom of Literature. How can we recognize Literature?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.II p.41-42 "There is another" - "ear or our eye."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION
How is the Beauty Sense used well?

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 22
lessons.body:
My Lord Intellect
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Why does Intellect need the Beauty Sense as a second companion? What service does the Beauty Sense provide?

Today’s reading uses the word "simulation." Do you know what a simulation is? [the imitation of a situation or a process]

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.II p.42-44 "But, like all" - "weary slave."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
marks: the British word for grades

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 23
lessons.body:
Daemons of Intellect
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
What do you remember about the Hall of Simulation? Why and how is it a threat to Mansoul? 

Today, we will read about the Daemons of Intellect. (Daemon is an old spelling for demon.) A daemon in our reading is a force, decision, or habit that takes something God created to be good and twists it into something unhealthy.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.III p.45-47 "Like the Body" - "royal and magnanimous."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
inertia: indisposition to motion, exertion, or change; the tendency to stay unmoving

jibs: to show hesitation or reluctance 

coster's barrow: the wheelbarrow/cart of a fruit and vegetable seller

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
My Lord Chief Explorer, Imagination
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Last term, we finished with a discussion about the Daemons of Intellect. Why is Habit considered one of these Daemons? What does it mean to have a Magnanimous Mind?

We will start our new term by learning more about the Lord Chief Explorer of Mansoul, Imagination. What do you recall about him from last term? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.IV p.48-51 "My Lord Chief" - "kind meantime!"
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
spinney: a small thatch of bushes or trees

fjord: a long, narrow sea inlet with steep sides or cliffs, created by a glacier

Sir Walter Scott: a Scottish novelist, poet, and playwright, and one of Mason's personal favorites

• COMPOSITION
When is Imagination a good servant? What does it act like when it is directed at Self?

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 26
lessons.body:
My Lord Chief Explorer, Imagination
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
In our last lesson, we had a more thorough introduction to Imagination. Why is Imagination important? Describe how it can be used unlawfully. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.IV p.51-53 "But the others" - "in your thoughts."

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 27
lessons.body:
The Beauty Sense
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Describe how to combat the Daemon of Self in regards to Imagination from our last lesson. Why must we take care to guard our imagination from unclean or horrible thoughts? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.V p.54-55 "The Beauty Sense" - "terror for us."

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 28
lessons.body:
Lord Chief Attorney-General, Reason
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
What do you recall from our last lesson about the Beauty Sense? How can we keep it in its right place? 

Today, we will delve into a more thorough discussion of the Lord Chief Attorney-General of Mansoul: Reason. An attorney general is a position that usually serves as the main legal advisor to the government.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.VI p.56-58 "I have spoken" - "secretly entertained."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
K.C.: the King's Counsel 

The Tempest: a famous play by William Shakespeare in which a Duke named Prospero is set adrift to die by his brother, Antonio. Prospero shirked his responsibility as Duke in pursuit of the knowledge of magic and philosophy.

• COMPOSITION
Describe Reason.

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.VI p.58-60 "The good man's" - "long ago settled."
lessons.teacherNotes:

John Howard: the first reformer of the British prison system

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 30
lessons.body:
Lord Chief Attorney-General, Reason
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
What do you remember about how a good man's Reason operates? Why is Reason important for invention and innovation?

Today, we will hear about what happens when Reason is viewed as the highest possible aim. 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.VI p.60-62 "It is very" - "the reasoning begins."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• COMPOSITION
Give some examples of Reason working its way out in your life or in your community.

lessons.body:
Lord Chief Attorney-General, Reason
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Describe the dangers of viewing Reason as infallible. How are two sensible people able to come to equally good but opposing conclusions on the same subject? 

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.VI p.62-65 "We have all been" - "anything they see."

lessons.title: 25m Ethics: Part 1 - Lesson 32
lessons.body:
Lords of the Exchequer, The Desires
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
Describe the relationship between Reason and the Will from our last lesson.

Today, we will explore the Desires, which Mason refers to as the Lords of the Exchequer. The Exchequer is a British name for the government department responsible for the oversight and collection of revenue; in other words, the Treasury.   

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.VII p.66-68 "We consider" - "duly balanced."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
approbation: approval

lessons.body:
Lords of the Exchequer, The Desires
⍞ Materials: Ourselves

➜ INTRO
What do you recall from our last lesson about how the natural desire for approval can serve us? How can it become the Daemons of Vanity and Fame?

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.VII p.68-70 "Another Desire" - "spend his money."
lessons.teacherNotes:
• DEFINITIONS
on his mettle: being ready or forced to prove your ability

➜ READ, NARRATE, & DISCUSS  
Pt.II Ch.VII p.70-72 "As before" - "for our own."
lessons.teacherNotes:

• COMPOSITION
